learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate comprehensive and well-founded knowledge and understanding of current trends and expectations related to assessment, and the nature of the full range of assessment strategies and approaches and how assessment and reporting inform planning of effective learning and teaching.

Demonstrate application of knowledge and skills to practice through the design of criteria and standards rubrics linked to learning objectives, and use of moderation practices to support consistent and comparable judgments of student learning.

Demonstrate understanding of a range of strategies for providing timely and appropriate feedback to students, reporting to students and parents/carers, and purpose of maintaining effective records of student achievement.
Demonstrate critical reflection on nature and use of large-scale systemic assessment data and capacity to interpret such data to inform effective planning and teaching.
Demonstrate clear, fluent, and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional and academic conventions.

assessments.task_description:
Context

In order to provide the conditions for students to learn and improve, teachers need to know how to analyse and develop effective assessment tasks. This is an integral component of productive teaching and learning.

Task

In tutorials, you have had discussions about a variety of different samples of assessment tasks and discussed the nature and validity of assessment practices more broadly. Drawing upon a sample secondary school assessment task collected during a previous practicum (or from an alternative source e.g. QCAA website) and ‘validation’ discussions about assessment practices more broadly, write an essay that analyses the nature and effectiveness of this assessment task. You will also need to draw upon relevant literature on the nature of assessment more broadly and specific assessment setting practices, including in relation to standards and criteria, to critique the nature of the assessment item.

This task is informed by current trends and expectations of assessment in secondary schools, and involves identifying, describing and explaining the common types of assessment strategies used in schools, including informal, formal, diagnostic, formative and summative.

Australian Professional Standards for Teachers:

Standard/Graduate level

5.1 Assess student learning

Demonstrate understanding of assessment strategies, including informal and formal, diagnostic, formative and summative approaches to assess student learning.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Moderation meeting [in tutorials]

In a tutorial, pre-service teachers will moderate an assessment rubric (criteria sheet) and task sheet as a group, using a set of guiding questions (protocal for analysing assessment tasks). They will then present the key responses to the questions from their moderation meeting in their tutorial. Pre-service teachers will then individually grade one piece of student work, and then participate in a group moderation meeting about that assessment piece. This moderation will also follow a set of guiding questions. They will then present the key responses to the questions in their tutorial. 

Individual Reflection:

Due Date: Within one week of completing moderation meeting

After their final moderation presentation, pre-service teachers will develop and submit a one page reflection on what they have learned about assessment moderation processes to date, and the importance of these processes in relation to developing not only effective assessment tasks, but also student learning. This reflection will also include consideration of the reporting strategies that may be used to inform parents and/or caregivers about the student’s progress to-date, and a rationale for why it is important to maintain effective records of student achievement.

Australian Professional Standards for Teachers:

Focus/Descriptor

5.3 Make consistent and comparable judgments

Demonstrate understanding of assessment moderation and its application to support consistent and comparable judgements of student learning.

5.5 Report on student achievement

Demonstrate understanding of a range of strategies for reporting to students and parents/carers and the purpose of keeping accurate and reliable records of student achievement.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of assessment without approved extension will result in a failing grade.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Practice-based research report: Analysing, moderating and using student feedback (data)

Context

Teachers are expected to analyse, moderate and use student assessment data to inform their teaching and learning practices, and they need to be able to do this skillfully. As a nascent teacher (who will soon be graduating), this means being able to:

(a)  analyse student assessment data (evidence of student learning)

(b)  participate in professional dialogue to moderate evidence of student learning, and feedback; and

(c)  integrate your understanding of student assessment data (evidence of student learning) to inform your classroom teaching practices.

However, you are not doing this in a vacuum; there is a plethora of professional and research literature on assessment practices and data more broadly; and in relation to school settings, to which you can refer to assist with such work.

Task

This assignment will take the form of a practice-based research report, in which you will take a sample of student assessment and provide evidence about how you have:

(a)  analysed this student’s work sample (assessment data/evidence of student learning);

(b)  outlined the nature of the feedback that will need to be provided to the student. This is a 2-step process in which you independently identify this feedback, and then engage in a moderation task (with 1 or more colleagues [in Task 2]) to develop a better understanding of the nature of the feedback that should be provided to the student;

(c)  outline the nature of the learning sequences and lessons you would then need to provide the student to enable them to address their learning needs, as identified in the feedback. 

For each stage of this process, you need to engage with and critically analyse relevant professional and academic literature about assessment data/evidence of student learning, and draw upon professional discussions with your colleagues about how to provide relevant assessment feedback to students. You will also need to draw upon relevant literature relating to moderation of assessment, and how these practices of analysing students’ work and moderating feedback can inform curriculum development (learning sequences and lesson plans).

Use the following subheadings in your final report (or develop your own in light of the nature of the task):

1.   Analysing the student work sample

2.   Valuing and validating student moderation

3.   Developing learning sequences and lessons

Tip:

  • Section 1 should also include reflections upon the nature of the student sample as a form of data in relation to other forms of data (including large scale standardized data), and literature on these varied forms of data.
  • Section 2 is more explicitly focused upon the nature and value of moderation processes, so draw upon your understandings of moderation in all its forms from across the course, including moderation of student assessment tasks [Task 1], but particularly moderation of student samples of work [Task 2].
  • In many ways, Section 3 serves as a synthesizing activity for Sections 1 and 2. The best reports will also incorporate relevant literature relating to curriculum development practices informed by a multifaceted conception of data use.
  • Use APA style referencing. The UQ library website contains a user guide
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic:
Lecture & Tutorial 1

 Introduction

In the first lecture and tutorials, preservice teachers learn about how assessment can be used to determine students’ prior knowledge and inform lesson planning. The lecture introduces different strategies for assessing what students know or think they know before beginning a new unit of work. This includes diagnostic assessment approaches to pitch introductory lessons at an appropriate level (CC 2.3.1 – taught). Learning outcomes: L01

Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Lecture & Tutorial 1 Introduction In the first lecture and tutorials, preservice teachers learn about how assessment can be used to determine students’ prior knowledge and inform lesson planning. The lecture introduces different strategies for assessing what students know or think they know before beginning a new unit of work. This includes diagnostic assessment approaches to pitch introductory lessons at an appropriate level (CC 2.3.1 – taught).

learning_activities.topic:
Lecture & Tutorial 2

Making judgements

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05

In Tutorials 2-7, which follow the same general structure, preservice teachers analyse summative assessment tasks, rubrics and student work samples collected from previous professional experience placements to determine student understandings of different concepts. They assess the validity of these assessments in measuring student mastery, evaluating them against curriculum standards and benchmarks. Based on their analyses, they collaboratively design introductory lessons that appropriately pitch new learning by addressing identified gaps and misconceptions. They consider how formative assessment strategies can be incorporated into these lessons to further refine their understanding of students' prior knowledge before commencing new units of work, and practise designing summative assessments to measure student learning, and analyse and improve assessment rubrics. Groups present their revised rubrics and justifications to the class, receiving peer feedback to further refine their understanding of how to create and use developmental rubrics (CC 2.3.1, 2.3.3, 2.3.4 – practised).

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Lecture and Tutorial 3

The Australian Curriculum and QCAA Assessment

Lectures 3 and 4 teach about using formative assessment to gather and interpret information about student learning. Strategies such as targeted oral questioning to assess student understanding in real time, are explored, helping to identify students’ misconceptions and guide their own instructional decisions. Examples of formative assessment practices are examined in the context of the Australian Curriculum and QCAA guidelines, aligned with curriculum expectations (CC 2.3.2 – taught). Lectures 3 and 4 teach how to provide effective feedback that is specific, honest, constructive, clear, and aligned with learning goals, and strategies for giving students timely, actionable feedback, including how to phrase feedback. The lectures cover explicit teaching strategies, such as scaffolding and targeted re-teaching, to address misconceptions and reinforce concepts being taught (CC 2.3.5 – taught).

Preservice teachers are taught to design and apply criteria and standards-based rubrics to support consistent and comparable judgments of student learning. The lectures explore how examples of well-structured rubrics from the Queensland Curriculum and Assessment Authority align with curriculum standards, clarify expectations for students, and provide a transparent framework for assessing student learning. Preservice teachers analyse how criteria and performance descriptors can be structured to help teachers assess student work against benchmarks (CC 2.3.3, 2.3.4 – taught). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

As interactive tasks during the lectures, students practise the use of formative assessment practices to gather and interpret information about student learning as learning is taking place (e.g., exit slips, quick quizzes, targeted oral questioning to prompt students to articulate their reasoning and identify common student misconceptions) (CC 2.3.2 – practised). Students will also have the opportunity to practise providing feedback as learning is taking place and how such feedback needs to be specific, honest, constructive, and clear, and which uses explicit teaching strategies to re-teach concepts, scaffold or correct misconceptions as necessary (CC 2.3.5 – practised). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Tutorial structure follows the description outlined for Tutorial 2

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
